USAGE:

    -c, --core <core>       Filename of coredump to display python stack traces from

py-spy-dump
Dumps stack traces for a target program to stdout

USAGE:
    py-spy dump [OPTIONS]

    -p, --pid <pid>         PID of a running python program to spy on
    -c, --core <core>       Filename of coredump to display python stack traces from
        --full-filenames    Show full Python filenames, instead of shortening to show only the package part
    -l, --locals            Show local variables for each frame. Passing multiple times (-ll) increases verbosity
    -j, --json              Format output as JSON
    -s, --subprocesses      Profile subprocesses of the original process

## Comments

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/402","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/402/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/402/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/402/events","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/community/issues/402","id":2594405180,"node_id":"I_kwDOC-0Tdc6ao3s8","number":402,"title":"Merge dask, distributed and dask-expr repos?","user":{"login":"fjetter","id":8629629,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2Mjk2Mjk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8629629?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter","html_url":"https://github.com/fjetter","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fjetter/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-10-17T11:14:25Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T08:52:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I frequently feel pain from having two distinct repositories with dask/dask and dask/distributed. Lately we've been working much more on changes that affect both repos and synchronizing PRs across repos is painful and cumbersome. With the addition of dask-expr this adds to a third repo and there are occasionally changes that span all three repos (e.g. sending Expr classes to the scheduler without materializing client side).

Additionally, documentation, maintenance and release procedures add additional work per repo.

The code is currently hard locked anyhow so we essentially sacrificed almost all flexibility of having multiple repos already and are pretty much paying for the disadvantage.

I would like to propose to merge the two (three) repos into a single one. We should still maintain multiple python packages so nothing would change for the end user other than having a single issue tracker to report issues to.

The problems I suspect we'll be running into are

- CI runtime for distributed is relatively high. We can remove some redundant tests once both are in the same repo but would still have longer runtime if everything is tested. We'd likely still maintain separate github workflows for testing that use appropriate [paths/paths-ignore](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions#onpushpull_requestpull_request_targetpathspaths-ignore) to somewhat decouple this.
- CI of distributed is flaky and has been for a long time. However, if the code only runs selectively depending on the path changes, this would not change by merging the repos
- This would likely impact all existing PRs since we'd likely have to change directory structure to support multiple `pyproject.toml` files


Are there problems I haven't thought about? Any other reasons why the two code bases should remain separate? I'm not very familiar with packaging. Is there anything in this realm that needs consideration?

cc @mrocklin @jacobtomlinson @quasiben @jrbourbeau @rjzamora @charlesbluca @hendrikmakait @phofl 

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/402/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/402/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/comments/2421870208","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/community/issues/402#issuecomment-2421870208","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/402","id":2421870208,"node_id":"IC_kwDOC-0Tdc6QWs6A","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-18T08:52:03Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T08:52:03Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> > When working on distirbuted I blame and bisect a lot to figure things out, so we would have to be careful not to lose the history. But bringing everything into one repo would definitely make bisecting easier.
> 
> Yes, I do that a lot, too. I would also only want to do this if we preserve the commit history of both repos. From the top of my head, I don't know how but I've done something similar in the past so it should be possible.

Merging git repos is definitely possible, see [this SO answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/10548919) for example. I gave it a quick try and much of it was successful, notably and not unexpectedly there are conflicts with files that exist in both repos, such as GH files, CI, some docs, `pyproject.toml`, etc., full list below.

<details><summary>Merge conflicts</summary>

```
.flake8
.git-blame-ignore-revs
.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md
.github/workflows/conda.yml
.github/workflows/publish-test-results.yaml
.github/workflows/release-drafter.yml
.github/workflows/release-publish.yml
.github/workflows/test-report.yaml
.gitignore
.pre-commit-config.yaml
.readthedocs.yaml
CODEOWNERS
CONTRIBUTING.md
LICENSE.txt
MANIFEST.in
README.rst
codecov.yml
conftest.py
continuous_integration/environment-3.10.yaml
continuous_integration/environment-3.11.yaml
continuous_integration/environment-3.12.yaml
continuous_integration/gpuci/build.sh
docs/Makefile
docs/make.bat
docs/release-procedure.md
docs/source/api.rst
docs/source/changelog.rst
docs/source/conf.py
docs/source/develop.rst
docs/source/faq.rst
docs/source/index.rst
docs/source/install.rst
docs/source/prometheus.rst
pyproject.toml
setup.py
```

</details>

So it would probably take someone knowledgeable of both repos at least a few hours to carefully go through conflicts carefully to prevent breaking anything, plus renaming files in their own directories. With all this said, merging doesn't look impossible for Dask+Distributed, if all the other aspects (like open issues, PRs, etc.) are resolved in a satisfactorily manner for everyone.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/community/issues/comments/2421870208/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Thank you for your  hard work.

I am evaluating 2d  zetas(twisted with harmonic polynomials): the lemniscate.
just sum over x,y for (x^4 - 6. * x^2 * y^2 + y^4)/(x^2+y^2)^4
I can do this in numba/cython/c/cuda up to 10**12 terms (1 000 000,1 000 000)

Now, with dask,  it crahes my WSL completly even for small values (100 000,100 000)

Here the GPU code (the CPU is just Client)

############################################

`import numpy as np
import cupy as cp

import dask.array as da
import dask

from dask_cuda import LocalCUDACluster
from dask.distributed import Client

cluster = LocalCUDACluster()
client = Client(cluster)
client

def zet(x,y):
    return ((x+1)**4 -6.*((x+1)**2)*((y+1)**2) + (y+1)**4)/((x+1)**2+(y+1)**2)**4

with dask.config.set({"array.backend": "cupy"}):
    xg = da.fromfunction(zet,shape=(50000,50000),dtype="float64")

%time xg.sum().compute()
`


############################################


It crahes for higher values of shape (100000,100000). Also when I put some higher values
of chunks (i have 6G gpu), it crashes.

The RAM consumption increases then it craches and the WSL terminal disapper

triage","color":"eeeeee","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-28T13:05:54Z","updated_at":"2024-10-28T14:00:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Describe the issue**:

This might be a bug in my code, but the behavior is dependent on dask, hence opening an issue here to confirm.

There are two issues here:
- The forward logging is not forwarded to the top-level client.
- It runs into a dead loop if the scheduler and the worker are freshly started. However, if I restart the worker without restarting the scheduler (Ctrl-C, then rerun the worker command), the log is simply lost.

output:
```
2024-10-28 20:54:29,748 - distributed.worker - INFO - -------------------------------------------------
2024-10-28 20:54:29,749 - distributed.core - INFO - Starting established connection to tcp://10.18.128.0:8786
2024-10-28 20:54:34,452 - distributed.worker - INFO - Starting Worker plugin forward-logging-test-logger
hello print
Hello
Hello
Hello
Hello
Hello
Hello
...
```

Currently, XGBoost logs the evaluation results on the scheduler process using its own communication channel. I'm trying to redirect the logs to the client process using Dask facilities.

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

Start a fresh cluster, this is required to reproduce the error.
``` sh
dask scheduler --scheduler-file=sched.json
dask worker --scheduler-file=sched.json
```

```python
from distributed import LocalCluster, Client, get_client
import logging


name = "test-logger"


def get_logger() -> logging.Logger:
    logger = logging.getLogger(name)
    logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
    if not logger.hasHandlers():
        handler = logging.StreamHandler()
        logger.addHandler(handler)
    return logger


def nested_fn():
    print("hello print", flush=True)
    get_logger().info("Hello")


def worker_fn():
    client = get_client()
    client.forward_logging(get_logger().name)
    client.submit(nested_fn).result()


def main():
    with Client(scheduler_file="sched.json") as client:
        client.submit(worker_fn).result()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

**Anything else we need to know?**:

**Environment**:

- Dask version: 2024.9.0
- Python version: 3.11.10
- Operating System: Ubuntu 22.04
- Install method (conda, pip, source): conda
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8915/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8915/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/comments/2441674309","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/issues/8915#issuecomment-2441674309","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8915","id":2441674309,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAocYk86RiP5F","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-28T14:00:35Z","updated_at":"2024-10-28T14:00:35Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"What happens here is that you submit `worker_fn()` that runs on each of the `Worker`s, in there you run [`get_client()`](https://distributed.dask.org/en/stable/api.html?highlight=get_client#distributed.get_client) which gets _a client_, that is a `Client` from the worker process and is _NOT_ the user-facing `Client`. I suspect what you want is to move `client.forward_logging(get_logger().name)` to your `main()` before submitting the task, e.g.:

```python
...
def worker_fn():
    client = get_client()
    client.submit(nested_fn).result()


def main():
    with Client(scheduler_file="sched.json") as client:
        client.forward_logging(get_logger().name)
        client.submit(worker_fn).result()
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/comments/2441674309/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

the issue**:

Two unexpected errors when calling `get_client` inside a function called by `client.sync`:
- The client becomes asynchronous even though the top-level client is synchronous.
- A runtime error and a type error are raised by Dask.

We use the `client.sync` method to handle asynchronous and synchronous environments uniformly in XGBoost. I'm not sure if this is the best way to write libraries, please share if there's a better approach.

Error message:
```

test_sync_call/asynchronous: True

  self.close()
/home/jiamingy/.anaconda/envs/xgboost_dev_125/lib/python3.11/site-packages/distributed/client.py:1730: RuntimeWarning: coroutine 'Client._close' was never awaited
  self.close()
Traceback (most recent call last):

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

```python
from distributed import LocalCluster, Client, get_client


def test_sync_call():
    with get_client() as client:
        print("test_sync_call/asynchronous:", client.asynchronous)



def main(client: Client) -> None:
    print("asynchronous:", client.asynchronous)
    client.sync(test_sync_call)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    with LocalCluster(n_workers=7, threads_per_worker=4) as cluster:
        with Client(cluster) as client:
            main(client)

```

However, [the docstring](https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/afa6e8dbd007b00f97e8dba6c4b1c4ad247a9a6e/distributed/utils.py#L352-L353) doesn't really define whether this is intentional or not, but the [`sync` function](https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/afa6e8dbd007b00f97e8dba6c4b1c4ad247a9a6e/distributed/utils.py#L388) which is called internally clarifies that `func` must be a coroutine. With that said, it seems to me that this is indeed intentional, everything that is called with `sync()` should return an awaitable, all existing tests assume that too. I'll not claim I know the reasoning behind that decision, but I suspect it is indeed intentional because without this it leaves the door open for users to write blocking code that may never allow the event loop to continue progressing and thus block the application indefinitely.

I think it's probably important to determine whether your function can be made asynchronous, and if not let us know the constraints here. Maybe Distributed maintainers who are more familiar with the internals would also have further comments on the `sync()` design and constraints, or suggestions how to move forward.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/comments/2442182353/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":11463,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/pulls/11463","id":2151300186,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAbcwm86AOjxa","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/11463","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/11463.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/11463.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/11463","number":11463,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Fix `Task.__repr__()` of unpickled object","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/11378 added a `Task._repr` object that is defined during `Task.__init__()`. In `Task.__repr__()` the object is tested but it doesn't account for the situation where `Task` may have been deserialized and thus `Task.__init__()` is never called, causing that to fail as the attribute was never defined. One other way to fix this would have been to define a default value when the attribute is defined in the scope of the class, but that unfortunately doesn't work due to the `__slots__ = tuple(__annotations__)` in the class. Another alternative to the `hasattr` check could be to define a `@property` with a different name and make `__repr__()` simply return that, which would handle checks regardless of `__init__()` being called, but I think that's too much bloat for little benefit.

added a `Task._repr` object that is defined during `Task.__init__()`. In `Task.__repr__()` the object is tested but it doesn't account for the situation where `Task` may have been deserialized and thus `Task.__init__()` is never called, causing that to fail as the attribute was never defined. One other way to fix this would have been to define a default value when the attribute is defined in the scope of the class, but that unfortunately doesn't work due to the `__slots__ = tuple(__annotations__)` in the class. Another alternative to the `hasattr` check could be to define a `@property` with a different name and make `__repr__()` simply return that, which would handle checks regardless of `__init__()` being called, but I think that's too much bloat for little benefit.

+;

solve is slower than `scipy` equivalent ","user":{"login":"jrbourbeau","id":11656932,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNjU2OTMy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11656932?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau","html_url":"https://github.com/jrbourbeau","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jrbourbeau/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":966951840,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5NjY5NTE4NDA=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/labels/cat:performance","name":"cat:performance","color":"84b6eb","default":false,"description":"Performance in terms of speed or memory consumption"},{"id":1052989469,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMDUyOTg5NDY5","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/labels/prio:medium","name":"prio:medium","color":"c686e0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-08-29T17:28:46Z","updated_at":"2024-09-16T07:56:43Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

While using `cupyx.scipy.sparse.linalg.splu`, I noticed that subsequent `solve()` steps using the `SuperLU` result returned from `cupyx.scipy.sparse.linalg.splu` are a lot slower (~120x slower in the example below) than the equivalent `scipy` operation on the CPU. 

### To Reproduce

Here's the `scipy` version (note I'm using the ipython/jupyter `%time` magic for timing the `linalg.splu` and `.solve` lines):

```py
import numpy as np
from scipy.sparse import random, csc_matrix
from scipy.sparse import linalg

size = 10_000
A = random(size, size, density=0.5, random_state=2)
A = csc_matrix(A)
x = np.random.default_rng(seed=2).random(size)
%time B = linalg.splu(A)    # 1min 10s
%time result = B.solve(x)   # 81.5 ms
```

The output here gives a runtime of 1min 10s for the `B = linalg.splu(A)` line and 81.5 ms for the `result = B.solve(x)` line.

Switching to the equivalent `cupy` version:

```python
import cupy as cp
from cupyx.scipy.sparse import random, csc_matrix
from cupyx.scipy.sparse import linalg

size = 10_000
A = random(size, size, density=0.5, random_state=2)
A = csc_matrix(A)
x = cp.random.default_rng(seed=2).random(size)
%time B = linalg.splu(A)    # 1min 15s
%time result = B.solve(x)   # 9.86 s
```

In this case, the `B = linalg.splu(A)` line takes ~1min 15s , which is similar to the `scipy` CPU version. This is expected as the docs https://docs.cupy.dev/en/stable/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.sparse.linalg.splu.html say that this step actually just happens on the CPU with `scipy` anyways. What's surprising is the `result = B.solve(x)` line, which happens on the GPU, takes 9.86 s, which much slower (~120x slower) than the equivalent `scipy` step. 


### Installation

Conda-Forge (`conda install ...`)

Description

While using `cupyx.scipy.sparse.linalg.splu`, I noticed that subsequent `solve()` steps using the `SuperLU` result returned from `cupyx.scipy.sparse.linalg.splu` are a lot slower (~120x slower in the example below) than the equivalent `scipy` operation on the CPU. 

### To Reproduce

Here's the `scipy` version (note I'm using the ipython/jupyter `%time` magic for timing the `linalg.splu` and `.solve` lines):

```py
import numpy as np
from scipy.sparse import random, csc_matrix
from scipy.sparse import linalg

size = 10_000
A = random(size, size, density=0.5, random_state=2)
A = csc_matrix(A)
x = np.random.default_rng(seed=2).random(size)
%time B = linalg.splu(A)    # 1min 10s
%time result = B.solve(x)   # 81.5 ms
```

The output here gives a runtime of 1min 10s for the `B = linalg.splu(A)` line and 81.5 ms for the `result = B.solve(x)` line.

Switching to the equivalent `cupy` version:

```python
import cupy as cp
from cupyx.scipy.sparse import random, csc_matrix
from cupyx.scipy.sparse import linalg

size = 10_000
A = random(size, size, density=0.5, random_state=2)
A = csc_matrix(A)
x = cp.random.default_rng(seed=2).random(size)
%time B = linalg.splu(A)    # 1min 15s
%time result = B.solve(x)   # 9.86 s
```

In this case, the `B = linalg.splu(A)` line takes ~1min 15s , which is similar to the `scipy` CPU version. This is expected as the docs https://docs.cupy.dev/en/stable/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.sparse.linalg.splu.html say that this step actually just happens on the CPU with `scipy` anyways. What's surprising is the `result = B.solve(x)` line, which happens on the GPU, takes 9.86 s, which much slower (~120x slower) than the equivalent `scipy` step. 


### Installation

Conda-Forge (`conda install ...`)

In either case, it is 1 feedstock, so I guess a rebuild.

If I understand correctly what repodata patching does it is essentially ensuring packages are still compatible (i.e., a package built with rdma-core 51 will have its metadata adjusted to make sure we can install and run with rdma-core 52), while this is totally valid to run against the new rdma-core (and probably even unnecessary, as packages like https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/main/recipe/meta.yaml do not pin to a specific version because compatibility is guaranteed) what this doesn't do is rebuild packages that use rdma-core to take advantage of any new features that may have been made available in the new rdma-core release, thus rebuilding is preferred even though it isn't required.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge-pinning-feedstock/issues/comments/2186893049/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

I'm trying to create a conda environment with dask and cupy-core. I'm relying on a site installation to provide the cuda libraries needed at runtime so I want to avoid having cuda-cudart installed in the conda environment. cupy-core has a dependency on cuda-version which seems fine but when I add dask to the environment it ends up pulling in cuda-cudart from ucx via libarrow-flight/pyarrow/dask.

I think the following is sufficient to reproduce my issue (although the thing I actually care about is dask + cupy-core):

```
conda create -p /tmp/env -c conda-forge ucx cuda-version
```

Installing ucx on its own does not pull in cuda-cudart so it seems like the presence of the cuda-version package is triggering this behavior. I tried to specify a cpu-only build of ucx with "ucx=*=*cpu" but the ucx version (1.6.1) seems a lot older than the latest available version so I suspect that may be old.

(I'm not sure this is a ucx problem, I suppose it could be related to how cupy-core or libarrow conda dependencies are specified but I figured I'd start here.)


### Installed packages

```shell

#

cuda-version              12.4                 h3060b56_3    conda-forge

Just for the sake of completeness, UCX does not support static linkage to cudart.



> IIRC UCX uses `dlopen` with all of the transports. So if they can't be resolved, it disables them. This is also true of the CUDA transports

This is correct.

> Potentially we could soften the `cuda-cudart` dependency. The one wrinkle is with `cuda-cudart` most packages statically link it, so users are less likely to get it. Plus they may not know to install it themselves. So if we do this, we may want to communicate it to users somehow

Making `cuda-cudart` optional is the only way to resolve this issue AFAIU. The implication, as John mentions, is that if the user forgets to install `cuda-cudart` then UCX will have GPU capabilities disabled and will fail when trying to transfer GPU objects.

> Static linking in the past led to some unpleasant issues. Believe these got fixed with PR ( [openucx/ucx#6038](https://github.com/openucx/ucx/pull/6038) ). That said, @pentschev would likely know the implications of static linking here better than I

We recently asked about this offline and the answer we got from UCX devs is that this isn't supported, so we shouldn't be doing that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/2076492091/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

I'm trying to create a conda environment with dask and cupy-core. I'm relying on a site installation to provide the cuda libraries needed at runtime so I want to avoid having cuda-cudart installed in the conda environment. cupy-core has a dependency on cuda-version which seems fine but when I add dask to the environment it ends up pulling in cuda-cudart from ucx via libarrow-flight/pyarrow/dask.

I think the following is sufficient to reproduce my issue (although the thing I actually care about is dask + cupy-core):

```
conda create -p /tmp/env -c conda-forge ucx cuda-version
```

Installing ucx on its own does not pull in cuda-cudart so it seems like the presence of the cuda-version package is triggering this behavior. I tried to specify a cpu-only build of ucx with "ucx=*=*cpu" but the ucx version (1.6.1) seems a lot older than the latest available version so I suspect that may be old.

(I'm not sure this is a ucx problem, I suppose it could be related to how cupy-core or libarrow conda dependencies are specified but I figured I'd start here.)

on user feedback, this softens the `cuda-cudart` dependency on CUDA 12. This matches the behavior on CUDA 11 where the `cudatoolkit` dependency is already softened

Includes a post-link script to explain to users how the enable CUDA support in UCX with specific install commands they can copy-paste

Also adds the error overlink check to test for missing dependencies. As GNU OpenMP is getting picked up and there is not a clear way to disable it without issues ( https://github.com/openucx/ucx/issues/9848 ) (despite OpenMP only being used in benchmarks and tests), this goes ahead and adds GNU OpenMP as a dependency. Hopefully we can revisit in the future

Fix #172

Comment:

After `rdma-core>49.0` is released, remove:

```
      ignore_run_exports:
        - rdma-core
```

and 

```
      run:
        - rdma-core >={{ rdma_core }}  # [linux64]
```

With https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/pull/13 having set `max_pin=None` it should suffice for UCX to simply rely on rdma-core's unrestricted upper bound. However, to avoid the need to mark rdma-core 49.0 build 0 as broken, we can wait for the next rdma-core release to remove the entries above.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/145/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/145/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

Comment:

After `rdma-core>49.0` is released, remove:

```
      ignore_run_exports:
        - rdma-core
```

and 

```
      run:
        - rdma-core >={{ rdma_core }}  # [linux64]
```

With https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/pull/13 having set `max_pin=None` it should suffice for UCX to simply rely on rdma-core's unrestricted upper bound. However, to avoid the need to mark rdma-core 49.0 build 0 as broken, we can wait for the next rdma-core release to remove the entries above.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/145/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/145/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"reopened"}}

This was only needed for `librdmacm` CDT, but that was dropped a while ago. So go ahead and drop `libnl` too

Lastly make sure `rdma` & `ibverbs` are always built (as we have `rdma-core` in all cases)

I understand that memory management in frowned up in Python, but sometimes you just have to do it.

`cp.asarray` seems to use a memory pool, but a mechanism to specify the data buffer where data should go seems like it would be beneficial, even in limited usecases.

could `cp.copyto` be expanded to provide at least preliminary support for well behaved numpy arrays (aligned, contiguous, anything else you can dream of)

### Additional Information

sometimes in troubleshooting you just want to know that you aren't hitting up any memory management issues.

I've been playing around with lazyarrays, and the ability to dispatch routines like `copyto` is pretty helpful.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8165/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8165/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/comments/2013144354","html_url":"https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/8165#issuecomment-2013144354","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8165","id":2013144354,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBFKgkM53_iUi","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-03-21T17:36:01Z","updated_at":"2024-03-21T17:36:01Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> @pentschev I recall you added the support for `CUPY_EXPERIMENTAL_SLICE_COPY` in #2079. lestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2023-11-13T10:53:23Z","updated_at":"2023-12-12T22:27:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"After filing #8337, I ran across `distributed.diagnostics.nvml`, which seems to be supposed to serve the same purpose of toggling _diagnotics_ on GPUs. However, after taking a deeper look, it seems as though this setting has some unintended consequences and disables the GPU executor altogether:

In `distributed.diagnostics.nvml`, it is used within `init_once` to decide whether to initialize `pynvml` on a process:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/diagnostics/nvml.py#L89-L93

The initialization is then checked within `device_get_count()`, which returns 0 if the toggle is turned off:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/diagnostics/nvml.py#L125-L128

On the worker, this function is then used to decide whether to initialize the GPU executor:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/worker.py#L639-L642

I'm not an expert on this particular area of code, but I think that we should separate initialization of `pynvml` from the diagnostics toggle. We may also want to consider moving diagnostics toggle into the system monitor (akin to #8337) to have a conceptual grouping.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8338/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8338/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/comments/1852912613","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/issues/8338#issuecomment-1852912613","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8338","id":1852912613,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAocYk85ucTPl","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-12T22:27:31Z","updated_at":"2023-12-12T22:27:31Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I had completely forgotten about those discussions, sorry about that. created","user":{"login":"hendrikmakait","id":2699097,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2OTkwOTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2699097?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait","html_url":"https://github.com/hendrikmakait","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":259867382,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyNTk4NjczODI=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"faadaf","default":true,"description":"Something triage","color":"eeeeee","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-11-13T10:53:23Z","updated_at":"2023-11-13T12:24:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"After filing #8337, I ran across `distributed.diagnostics.nvml`, which seems to be supposed to serve the same purpose of toggling _diagnotics_ on GPUs. However, after taking a deeper look, it seems as though this setting has some unintended consequences and disables the GPU executor altogether:

In `distributed.diagnostics.nvml`, it is used within `init_once` to decide whether to initialize `pynvml` on a process:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/diagnostics/nvml.py#L89-L93

The initialization is then checked within `device_get_count()`, which returns 0 if the toggle is turned off:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/diagnostics/nvml.py#L125-L128

On the worker, this function is then used to decide whether to initialize the GPU executor:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/worker.py#L639-L642

I'm not an expert on this particular area of code, but I think that we should separate initialization of `pynvml` from the diagnostics toggle. We may also want to consider moving diagnostics toggle into the system monitor (akin to #8337) to have a conceptual can speak for the NVML diagnostics side, and we intentionally chose to decide whether to enable diagnostics or not when `device_get_count() == 0` which may indeed cause some confusion as it packs more information than its name suggests:

1. Is the NVML diagnostics toggle turned on?
2. Is `pynvml` installed?
3. Are NVML resources actually available (e.g., shared libraries)?
4. And finally, are there GPUs installed and visible?

We could separate all of those things but it adds a ton of boilerplate code that's easy to miss, even just having to test whether NVML is enabled and the device count is, from previous experience, easy enough to introduce unintended consequences. With that said, so far we assumed that if GPU diagnostics are disabled, everything GPU-related is intended to be disabled.

I wouldn't mind making changes so that we can still query whether GPUs are available even if the NVML toggle is off if there's use for you. I was not aware of the existence of `Dask-GPU-Threads` executor and I'm not familiar with its purpose, in Dask-CUDA we do not use it even though we rely heavily on the Distributed NVML diagnostics module. With that said, if having another path that can still keep `Dask-GPU-Threads` enabled despite NVML diagnostics switched off I'm ok with that, I would still like to retain the current behavior of `device_get_count() == 0` if NVML diagnostics are switched off though, and we can then perhaps introduce a new function or `bool` argument to `device_get_count` to still return the device count independent of the NVML diagnostics toggle.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/comments/1808068609/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

created","user":{"login":"hendrikmakait","id":2699097,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2OTkwOTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2699097?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait","html_url":"https://github.com/hendrikmakait","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hendrikmakait/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":259867382,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyNTk4NjczODI=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"faadaf","default":true,"description":"Something triage","color":"eeeeee","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-11-13T10:53:23Z","updated_at":"2023-11-13T12:53:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"After filing #8337, I ran across `distributed.diagnostics.nvml`, which seems to be supposed to serve the same purpose of toggling _diagnotics_ on GPUs. However, after taking a deeper look, it seems as though this setting has some unintended consequences and disables the GPU executor altogether:

In `distributed.diagnostics.nvml`, it is used within `init_once` to decide whether to initialize `pynvml` on a process:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/diagnostics/nvml.py#L89-L93

The initialization is then checked within `device_get_count()`, which returns 0 if the toggle is turned off:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/diagnostics/nvml.py#L125-L128

On the worker, this function is then used to decide whether to initialize the GPU executor:

https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/0dc9e8800d6e49e77e2fd5cbc71f0033cccf7b3b/distributed/worker.py#L639-L642

I'm not an expert on this particular area of code, but I think that we should separate initialization of `pynvml` from the diagnostics toggle. We may also want to consider moving diagnostics toggle into the system monitor (akin to #8337) to have a conceptual grouping.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8338/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8338/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/comments/1808110910","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/issues/8338#issuecomment-1808110910","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8338","id":1808110910,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAocYk85rxZU-","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-13T12:53:29Z","updated_at":"2023-11-13T12:53:29Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> This makes less sense to me. Shouldn't it instead be the other way around? If GPUs are generally enabled/disabled, then diagnostics should follow suit (possibly with the additional ability to turn off diagnostics either way).

Yes, I would agree with that, but Distributed had no GPU code (at least to my knowledge) other than monitoring, thus we were making the assumption in the opposite direction (diagnostics disabled == intent to have no GPU support).

> A more general question: Are NVML diagnostics independent of the system monitor? If not, I'm wondering if we should move the toggle there to be close to the other system monitor toggles `distributed.admin.system-monitor.{disk|host-cpu|gil}`.

I don't mind moving the toggle, I think it was introduced in the NVML diagnostics module just to be the least intrusive as possible, if you see benefit in having it as part of system monitor I'm +1 for that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/comments/1808110910/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`max_pin` to `None`?","user":{"login":"jakirkham","id":3019665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMwMTk2NjU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3019665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham","html_url":"https://github.com/jakirkham","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-11-22T22:01:09Z","updated_at":"2023-11-28T14:08:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Based on the discussion in issue ( https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143 ), it sounds like we are comfortable not having a `max_pin` for `rdma-core`

Currently the default `max_pin` is used, which is equivalent to `max_pin="x"`. IOW the maximum allow version is the next major version

https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/blob/1f2ee3bfe47659c36637cdf5b9191f2426efee68/recipe/meta.yaml#L17-L18

However given the high level compatibility from `rdma-core` and the frequency of major versions, wonder if we should use `max_pin=None`, meaning that there would be no `max_pin` for `rdma-core`

```diff
   run_exports:
-    - {{ pin_subpackage("rdma-core") }}
+    - {{ pin_subpackage("rdma-core", max_pin=None) }}
```

Thoughts?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/12/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/12/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/comments/1829917563","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/12#issuecomment-1829917563","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/12","id":1829917563,"node_id":"IC_kwDOJmIC2c5tElN7","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-28T14:08:48Z","updated_at":"2023-11-28T14:08:48Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I agree with Keith that given there's strong commitment to ABI compatibility it should be mostly fine to remove the `max_pin`. However, I've recently seen cases where not having something equivalent to `max_pin` can be problematic, it's not even a conda issue but I wonder if something similar could occur here.

The situation I'm referring to above involves `dask-cuda` PyPI packages that didn't specify an upper bound of `dask` compatibility, so what happens today is if you're installing a large set of PyPI packages among which `dask-cuda` is included _without specifying the version_ (which intends to pick the latest), then an old version of `dask-cuda` will be installed instead. This happens because latest `dask-cuda` pins to a specific version of `dask` that is not the latest and some other package wants to pick latest `dask`, thus the solver ends up picking up a very old `dask-cuda` version that allows installing latest `dask`.

Could we fall under a similar problem here if we don't specify a `max_pin` _and_ one day `rdma-core` needs to break ABI?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/comments/1829917563/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`None`?","user":{"login":"jakirkham","id":3019665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMwMTk2NjU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3019665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham","html_url":"https://github.com/jakirkham","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-11-22T22:01:09Z","updated_at":"2023-11-30T13:55:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Based on the discussion in issue ( https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143 ), it sounds like we are comfortable not having a `max_pin` for `rdma-core`

Currently the default `max_pin` is used, which is equivalent to `max_pin="x"`. IOW the maximum allow version is the next major version

https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/blob/1f2ee3bfe47659c36637cdf5b9191f2426efee68/recipe/meta.yaml#L17-L18

However given the high level compatibility from `rdma-core` and the frequency of major versions, wonder if we should use `max_pin=None`, meaning that there would be no `max_pin` for `rdma-core`

```diff
   run_exports:
-    - {{ pin_subpackage("rdma-core") }}
+    - {{ pin_subpackage("rdma-core", max_pin=None) }}
```

Thoughts?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/12/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/12/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/comments/1833829046","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/12#issuecomment-1833829046","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/issues/12","id":1833829046,"node_id":"IC_kwDOJmIC2c5tTgK2","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-30T13:55:20Z","updated_at":"2023-11-30T13:55:20Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"That's good to hear. Given rdma-core has a strong commitment to maintain ABI compatibility, it seems reasonable to not enforce a `max_pin`. Comment:

After `rdma-core>49.0` is released, remove:

```
      ignore_run_exports:
        - rdma-core
```

and 

```
      run:
        - rdma-core >={{ rdma_core }}  # [linux64]
```

With https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/pull/13 having set `max_pin=None` it should suffice for UCX to simply rely on rdma-core's unrestricted upper bound. However, to avoid the need to mark rdma-core 49.0 build 0 as broken, we can wait for the next rdma-core release to remove the entries above.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/145/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/145/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

requested"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-10-09T19:44:28Z","updated_at":"2023-10-10T07:00:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Comment:

Based on previous discussions we were under the false impression that rdma-core 28.x was necessary to support all of MOFED 5.x. That understanding was incorrect, even though rdma-core 28.x should still support MOFED 5.x moving to the latest rdma-core (currentl 48.0) should be preferred instead, and the newest rdma-core version will still support MOFED 5.x. The newest rdma-core should also support all modern kernel versions, so this should not be a blocker for most users either.

With the above said, we can make a choice here as to what versions we should pin UCX to, just earlier today I [released rdma-core=48.0](https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/pull/8), it seems that we need to build UCX against the rdma-core version that contains all features we want/need, or in other words, if we build against the newest rdma-core we should support all features that UCX can utilize from rdma-core. However, we are still capable of running against newer rdma-core core versions, so do we want to pin UCX builds tightly to the current major version or do we want to have a loose pin to support newer rdma-core versions?

Advantages of pinning to `rdma-core>=48.0`:

- Does not force us to rebuild immediately against new rdma-core versions;
- Permits users who need a specific UCX version to upgrade to a newer rdma-core runtime.

Disadvantages:

- We can't guarantee that compatibility will be forever valid;
  - We may potentially want to define a maximum version (maybe arbitrarily define 3 or 5 major versions above?);
- Slightly more complex to maintain (needs to keep the [version to build against in CBC](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/main/recipe/conda_build_config.yaml#L1-L2), and add the pins we support to [run requirements](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/main/recipe/meta.yaml#L42)).

With the above said, I'd like to ask all people who care about InfiniBand/rdma-core support to comment on what sounds like the best approach to follow here, so please let me know if you have strong opinions about this. If we have no comments or strong opinions I will propose we move ahead with merging https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/pull/142 which will implicitly pin to `rdma-core>=48,<49` at runtime.

cc @jakirkham @kkraus14 @SeyedMir as you are some of the usual suspects and users on rdma-core matters.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1754538974","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143#issuecomment-1754538974","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143","id":1754538974,"node_id":"IC_kwDODBZhKc5olCPe","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-10-10T07:00:05Z","updated_at":"2023-10-10T07:00:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> This will allow upgrades of `rdma-core` to be handled with migrations and also ensure it is pinned consistently throughout `conda-forge`

requested"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":10,"created_at":"2023-10-09T19:44:28Z","updated_at":"2023-10-11T09:35:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Comment:

Based on previous discussions we were under the false impression that rdma-core 28.x was necessary to support all of MOFED 5.x. That understanding was incorrect, even though rdma-core 28.x should still support MOFED 5.x moving to the latest rdma-core (currentl 48.0) should be preferred instead, and the newest rdma-core version will still support MOFED 5.x. The newest rdma-core should also support all modern kernel versions, so this should not be a blocker for most users either.

With the above said, we can make a choice here as to what versions we should pin UCX to, just earlier today I [released rdma-core=48.0](https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/pull/8), it seems that we need to build UCX against the rdma-core version that contains all features we want/need, or in other words, if we build against the newest rdma-core we should support all features that UCX can utilize from rdma-core. However, we are still capable of running against newer rdma-core core versions, so do we want to pin UCX builds tightly to the current major version or do we want to have a loose pin to support newer rdma-core versions?

Advantages of pinning to `rdma-core>=48.0`:

- Does not force us to rebuild immediately against new rdma-core versions;
- Permits users who need a specific UCX version to upgrade to a newer rdma-core runtime.

Disadvantages:

- We can't guarantee that compatibility will be forever valid;
  - We may potentially want to define a maximum version (maybe arbitrarily define 3 or 5 major versions above?);
- Slightly more complex to maintain (needs to keep the [version to build against in CBC](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/main/recipe/conda_build_config.yaml#L1-L2), and add the pins we support to [run requirements](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/main/recipe/meta.yaml#L42)).

With the above said, I'd like to ask all people who care about InfiniBand/rdma-core support to comment on what sounds like the best approach to follow here, so please let me know if you have strong opinions about this. If we have no comments or strong opinions I will propose we move ahead with merging https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/pull/142 which will implicitly pin to `rdma-core>=48,<49` at runtime.

cc @jakirkham @kkraus14 @SeyedMir as you are some of the usual suspects and users on rdma-core matters.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1757267089","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143#issuecomment-1757267089","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/143","id":1757267089,"node_id":"IC_kwDODBZhKc5ovcSR","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-10-11T09:35:58Z","updated_at":"2023-10-11T09:35:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Thanks Keith for the thorough explanation. Based on your reply I've submitted https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge-pinning-feedstock/pull/5017 to add `rdma-core` to the global pinnings. Besides that, I've also [added `rdma-core>=48` to the UCX recipe](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/pull/142/commits/eb2bd9a4b214c709322795aa0e92349d48da1244).

Some things that aren't clear to me yet:

1. Do we need to add a `max_pin` to [`rdma-core`'s recipe](https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/blob/b617c8a772a849dd5e0b4026f4b56ca221941bce/recipe/meta.yaml#L18)? If we do, I imagine we want `max_pin="x.x"`, just like UCX utilizes, could you confirm that?
2. To this day I remain confused about `run`/`run_exports`/`run_constrained`, could you confirm if we want to add `rdma-core` to `run_exports` as done via [this commit](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/pull/142/commits/eb2bd9a4b214c709322795aa0e92349d48da1244), or if we should move it to `run`/`run_constrained` instead?
3. Do we still need a [`conda_build_config.yaml` file](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/eb2bd9a4b214c709322795aa0e92349d48da1244/recipe/conda_build_config.yaml) to include the `rdma-core` pin, or is that now satisfied given we're moving it to a global pinning? IOW, can we remove that file completely now from the UCX recipe?

pinning","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1504562902,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNTA0NTYyOTAy","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/labels/question","name":"question","color":"d876e3","default":true,"description":"Further requested"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":16,"created_at":"2023-10-09T19:44:28Z","updated_at":"2023-10-11T19:46:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Comment:

Based on previous discussions we were under the false impression that rdma-core 28.x was necessary to support all of MOFED 5.x. That understanding was incorrect, even though rdma-core 28.x should still support MOFED 5.x moving to the latest rdma-core (currentl 48.0) should be preferred instead, and the newest rdma-core version will still support MOFED 5.x. The newest rdma-core should also support all modern kernel versions, so this should not be a blocker for most users either.

With the above said, we can make a choice here as to what versions we should pin UCX to, just earlier today I [released rdma-core=48.0](https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/pull/8), it seems that we need to build UCX against the rdma-core version that contains all features we want/need, or in other words, if we build against the newest rdma-core we should support all features that UCX can utilize from rdma-core. However, we are still capable of running against newer rdma-core core versions, so do we want to pin UCX builds tightly to the current major version or do we want to have a loose pin to support newer rdma-core versions?

Advantages of pinning to `rdma-core>=48.0`:

- Does not force us to rebuild immediately against new rdma-core versions;
- Permits users who need a specific UCX version to upgrade to a newer rdma-core runtime.

Disadvantages:

- We can't guarantee that compatibility will be forever valid;
  - We may potentially want to define a maximum version (maybe arbitrarily define 3 or 5 major versions above?);
- Slightly more complex to maintain (needs to keep the [version to build against in CBC](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/main/recipe/conda_build_config.yaml#L1-L2), and add the pins we support to [run requirements](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/main/recipe/meta.yaml#L42)).

With the above said, I'd like to ask all people who care about InfiniBand/rdma-core support to comment on what sounds like the best approach to follow here, so please let me know if you have strong opinions about this. If we have no comments or strong opinions I will propose we move ahead with merging https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/pull/142 which will implicitly pin to `rdma-core>=48,<49` at runtime.

cc @jakirkham @kkraus14 @SeyedMir as you are some of the usual suspects and users on rdma-core All of that is correct, but in addition to that I believe we need to ignore the run export from rdma-core via the `ignore_run_exports` (or `ignore_run_exports_from` section: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda-build/en/stable/resources/define-metadata.html#export-runtime-requirements
> 
> Otherwise, I believe you would end up with both the run export constraint and the defined constraint, which when combined yields the same behavior as just the run export constraint.

Thanks Keith, given we want to ignore only `rdma-core` from `run_exports` -- and if my understanding is correct --, I think the appropriate is to add it to the `ignore_run_exports` and not `ignore_run_exports_from` which would include _all_ `rdma-core`'s `run_exports` (although none other than `rdma-core` itself are currently defined). I've [pushed that change](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/pull/142/commits/1e78eaf356aeb77ee55d8238fefc7a70fda7b4d3), but please feel free to correct or disagree with my understanding if you think `ignore_run_exports_from` would be appropriate.

> Linking this comment here in case it was missed: [conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock#9 (comment)](https://github.com/conda-forge/rdma-core-feedstock/pull/9#discussion_r1355590069)

{"action":"opened","number":8272,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/pulls/8272","id":1555800723,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAocYk85cu6KT","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/pull/8272","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/pull/8272.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/pull/8272.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/8272","number":8272,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Reenable `test_ucx_config_w_env_var`","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Some time ago `test_ucx_config_w_env_var` started failing intermittently, and the causes were still unknown. After some investigation it seems in certain cases exchanging UCX-Py peer information causes some of the underlying communication calls to never complete and thus cause a hang that can't be recovered from by Distributed. With https://github.com/rapidsai/ucx-py/pull/994, UCX-Py now has a timeout on those calls that allow Distributed to catch and retry establishing the connection, which seems to resolve the problem.

Closes #5229

time ago `test_ucx_config_w_env_var` started failing intermittently, and the causes were still unknown. After some investigation it seems in certain cases exchanging UCX-Py peer information causes some of the underlying communication calls to never complete and thus cause a hang that can't be recovered from by Distributed. With https://github.com/rapidsai/ucx-py/pull/994, UCX-Py now has a timeout on those calls that allow Distributed to catch and retry establishing the connection, which seems to resolve the problem.

Closes #5229

Exposes cuDF's [spilling statistics](https://docs.rapids.ai/api/cudf/stable/developer_guide/library_design/#statistics) as an extra worker metric, and refactors the RMM/GPU memory plot to reflect this information in a similar way to the standard worker memory plot.

It seems that we've now lost the [opacity for GPU memory used/RMM pool size/used memory size](https://github.com/dask/distributed/pull/7718#issue-1645863692), or is it simply the case that you didn't have a pool enable and thus it didn't show?

> Note that the color scheme follows Dask defaults because I'm using `MemoryColor` to control the bar's coloring, if this is a problem it shouldn't be too difficult to modify that class to allow for overriding the default color scheme.

I think the green tones were @jacobtomlinson 's idea, they provide a nice touch for GPU memory, particularly I think it looked nicer as well. I'd anyway defer visual details to Jacob anyway, I think he's back Monday anyway. 🙂 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/comments/1703289132/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/pulls/comments/1313491125","pull_request_review_id":1607638912,"id":1313491125,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAocYk85OSki1","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,25 @@
+"""
+Diagnostics for memory spilling managed by cuDF.
+"""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+try:
+    from cudf.core.buffer.spill_manager import get_global_manager
+except ImportError:
+    get_global_manager = None
+
+
+def real_time():
+    if get_global_manager is None:
+        return {"cudf-spilled": 0}
+    mgr = get_global_manager()
+    if mgr is None:
+        return {"cudf-spilled": 0}
+
+    totals = mgr.statistics.spill_totals
+
+    return {
+        "cudf-spilled": totals.get(("gpu", "cpu"), (0,))[0]
+        - totals.get(("cpu", "gpu"), Exposes cuDF's [spilling statistics](https://docs.rapids.ai/api/cudf/stable/developer_guide/library_design/#statistics) as an extra worker metric, and refactors the RMM/GPU memory plot to reflect this information in a similar way to the standard worker memory plot.

cc @pentschev @quasiben 

- [ ] Tests added / passed
- [ ] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`
