documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-09-13T10:33:32Z","updated_at":"2023-09-20T12:21:43Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/pulls/10508","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/10508","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/10508.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/10508.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I've recently started working more with Dask on HPC and thought it is a good opportunity to update the docs page.

High-level changes:
- Improve Dask MPI section.
- Discuss the differences in deployment model between dask-jobqueue and dask-mpi more thoroughly.
- Add Dask Gateway section.
- Update the Jupyter section to use the new `--jupyter` flag.
- General hierarchy updates.

It might also be nice to update the High Performance Network section to talk about UCX, but I could only find NVIDIA/GPU specific examples. It would be good to be more generic here and just talk about IB. cc @pentschev in case you have thoughts

I also haven't added a GPU section, but it might be useful to folks and we could talk at a high level and then direct them to RAPIDS docs for this. Wanted to check with folks like @mrocklin, @jrbourbeau, @fjetter before doing this. I can always add this in a follow up.

cc @kmpaul for dask-mpi","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/10508/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/10508/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1727615128","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/10508#issuecomment-1727615128","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/10508","id":1727615128,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAbcwm85m-VCY","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-20T12:21:42Z","updated_at":"2023-09-20T12:21:42Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> It might also be nice to update the High Performance Network section to talk about UCX, but I could only find NVIDIA/GPU specific examples. It would be good to be more generic here and just talk about IB. cc @pentschev in case you have thoughts

This may be useful, I'm only not sure if there are any non-GPU InfiniBand users out there currently. We can always say using `protocol="ucx"` will enable InfiniBand support (although IPoIB may be available for those machines where IB interfaces have proper routing/IP addresses configured even without UCX and the user explicitly identifies the `ibX` network interface for comms), but I'm not currently aware of any CPU-only benchmarking either, although I think this is something we could do.

Do you have something more specific in mind you would like to have documented other than the general guidelines I mentioned above?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1727615128/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-09-13T10:33:32Z","updated_at":"2023-09-20T19:53:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/pulls/10508","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/10508","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/10508.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/10508.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I've recently started working more with Dask on HPC and thought it is a good opportunity to update the docs page.

High-level changes:
- Improve Dask MPI section.
- Discuss the differences in deployment model between dask-jobqueue and dask-mpi more thoroughly.
- Add Dask Gateway section.
- Update the Jupyter section to use the new `--jupyter` flag.
- General hierarchy updates.

It might also be nice to update the High Performance Network section to talk about UCX, but I could only find NVIDIA/GPU specific examples. It would be good to be more generic here and just talk about IB. cc @pentschev in case you have thoughts

I also haven't added a GPU section, but it might be useful to folks and we could talk at a high level and then direct them to RAPIDS docs for this. Wanted to check with folks like @mrocklin, @jrbourbeau, @fjetter before doing this. I can always add this in a follow up.

cc @kmpaul for dask-mpi","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/10508/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/10508/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1728339989","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/10508#issuecomment-1728339989","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/10508","id":1728339989,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAbcwm85nBGAV","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-20T19:53:34Z","updated_at":"2023-09-20T19:53:34Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> @pentschev there are definitely folks out there using IPoIB as many docs pages and examples show how to set your interface to the IB one. I just wondered what the current state of non-GPU UCX in Dask is and whether we should point folks to it?

I'm not familiar with any technical shortcomings, for all I know it should all "just work". Pointing users to it seems like a great idea, we would essentially need to tell them to use `protocol="ucx"` and specify `--interface` pointing scheduler and workers to an IB interface, that should be all. The only point I was trying to make previously is that we (RAPIDS) haven't tested CPU-only workflows with IB, but there's no reason to believe they wouldn't work.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1728339989/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import pytest
+
+from distributed.utils_test import gen_cluster
+
+pytestmark = pytest.mark.gpu
+
+cudf = pytest.importorskip("cudf")
+dask_cuda = pytest.importorskip("dask_cuda")
+
+
+def force_spill():
+    from cudf.core.buffer.spill_manager import get_global_manager
+
+    manager = get_global_manager()
+
+    # 24 bytes
+    df = cudf.DataFrame({"a": [1, 2, 3]})
+
+    return manager.spill_to_device_limit(1)
+
+

https://github.com/rapidsai/dask-cuda/issues/1226

Exposes cuDF's [spilling statistics](https://docs.rapids.ai/api/cudf/stable/developer_guide/library_design/#statistics) as an extra worker metric, and refactors the RMM/GPU memory plot to reflect this information in a similar way to the standard worker memory plot.

cc @pentschev @quasiben

- [ ] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

Please be concise with code posted. See guidelines below on how to provide a good bug report:

- Craft Minimal Bug Reports http://matthewrocklin.com/blog/work/2018/02/28/minimal-bug-reports
- Minimal Complete Verifiable Examples https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve

Bug reports that follow these guidelines are easier to diagnose, and so are often handled much more quickly.
-->

**Describe the issue**:
Passing `dashboard_address=None` to `LocalCluster()` does not disable dashboard, even though the [`LocalCluster` documentation](https://docs.dask.org/en/latest/deploying-python.html?highlight=localcluster#reference) states `None` should disable it.

Is this a bug or intended change with outdated documentation?

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

```python
In [1]: from distributed import LocalCluster

In [2]: cluster1 = LocalCluster()

In [3]: cluster2 = LocalCluster(dashboard_address=None)
/datasets/pentschev/miniconda3/envs/rn-230828/lib/python3.9/site-packages/distributed/node.py:182: UserWarning: Port 8787 is already in use.
Perhaps you already have a cluster running?
Hosting the HTTP server on port 44165 instead
  warnings.warn(
```

**Anything else we need to know?**:
It's unclear whether that's still the intended behavior. I found no tests or even uses for `dashboard_address=None` anywhere in the codebase, I would expect tests to use `dashboard_address=None` to prevent warnings such as the one below, but all tests use `dashboard_address=":0"` (bind to a random port) instead to prevent warnings that the dashboard port is already in use.

<details><summary>Probable fix</summary>

```patch
diff --git a/distributed/scheduler.py b/distributed/scheduler.py
index 0937e5b2..d8776b63 100644
--- a/distributed/scheduler.py
+++ b/distributed/scheduler.py
@@ -3529,6 +3529,7 @@ class Scheduler(SchedulerState, ServerNode):
             default_port=self.default_port,
         )

+        self.jupyter = jupyter
         http_server_modules = dask.config.get("distributed.scheduler.http.routes")
         show_dashboard = dashboard or (dashboard is None and dashboard_address)
         # install vanilla route if show_dashboard but bokeh is not installed
@@ -3538,15 +3539,15 @@ class Scheduler(SchedulerState, ServerNode):
             except ImportError:
                 show_dashboard = False
                 http_server_modules.append("distributed.http.scheduler.missing_bokeh")
-        routes = get_handlers(
-            server=self, modules=http_server_modules, prefix=http_prefix
-        )
-        self.start_http_server(routes, dashboard_address, default_port=8787)
-        self.jupyter = jupyter
-        if show_dashboard:
+
+            routes = get_handlers(
+                server=self, modules=http_server_modules, prefix=http_prefix
+            )
+            self.start_http_server(routes, dashboard_address, default_port=8787)
             distributed.dashboard.scheduler.connect(
                 self.http_application, self.http_server, self, prefix=http_prefix
             )
+
         if self.jupyter:
             try:
                 from jupyter_server.serverapp import ServerApp
```
</details>

<details><summary>Reproducer with patch above applied</summary>

```python
In [1]: from distributed import LocalCluster

In [2]: cluster1 = LocalCluster()

In [3]: cluster2 = LocalCluster(dashboard_address=None)

In [4]:
```

</details>

**Environment**:

- Dask version: dask=2023.8.2a230825, distributed=2023.8.2a230825
- Python version: 3.9.17
- Operating System: Ubuntu 20.04
- Install method (conda, pip, source): conda

Bug reports that follow these guidelines are easier to diagnose, and so are often handled much more quickly.

**Describe the issue**:
Passing `dashboard_address=None` to `LocalCluster()` does not disable dashboard, even though the [`LocalCluster` documentation](https://docs.dask.org/en/latest/deploying-python.html?highlight=localcluster#reference) states `None` should disable it.

Is this a bug or intended change with outdated documentation?

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

```python
In [1]: from distributed import LocalCluster

In [2]: cluster1 = LocalCluster()

In [3]: cluster2 = LocalCluster(dashboard_address=None)
/datasets/pentschev/miniconda3/envs/rn-230828/lib/python3.9/site-packages/distributed/node.py:182: UserWarning: Port 8787 is already in use.
Perhaps you already have a cluster running?
Hosting the HTTP server on port 44165 instead
  warnings.warn(
```

**Anything else we need to know?**:
It's unclear whether that's still the intended behavior. I found no tests or even uses for `dashboard_address=None` anywhere in the codebase, I would expect tests to use `dashboard_address=None` to prevent warnings such as the one below, but all tests use `dashboard_address=":0"` (bind to a random port) instead to prevent warnings that the dashboard port is already in use.

<details><summary>Probable fix</summary>

```patch
diff --git a/distributed/scheduler.py b/distributed/scheduler.py
index 0937e5b2..d8776b63 100644
--- a/distributed/scheduler.py
+++ b/distributed/scheduler.py
@@ -3529,6 +3529,7 @@ class Scheduler(SchedulerState, ServerNode):
             default_port=self.default_port,
         )

+        self.jupyter = jupyter
         http_server_modules = dask.config.get("distributed.scheduler.http.routes")
         show_dashboard = dashboard or (dashboard is None and dashboard_address)
         # install vanilla route if show_dashboard but bokeh is not installed
@@ -3538,15 +3539,15 @@ class Scheduler(SchedulerState, ServerNode):
             except ImportError:
                 show_dashboard = False
                 http_server_modules.append("distributed.http.scheduler.missing_bokeh")
-        routes = get_handlers(
-            server=self, modules=http_server_modules, prefix=http_prefix
-        )
-        self.start_http_server(routes, dashboard_address, default_port=8787)
-        self.jupyter = jupyter
-        if show_dashboard:
+
+            routes = get_handlers(
+                server=self, modules=http_server_modules, prefix=http_prefix
+            )
+            self.start_http_server(routes, dashboard_address, default_port=8787)
             distributed.dashboard.scheduler.connect(
                 self.http_application, self.http_server, self, prefix=http_prefix
             )
+
         if self.jupyter:
             try:
                 from jupyter_server.serverapp import ServerApp
```
</details>

<details><summary>Reproducer with patch above applied</summary>

```python
In [1]: from distributed import LocalCluster

In [2]: cluster1 = LocalCluster()

In [3]: cluster2 = LocalCluster(dashboard_address=None)

In [4]:
```

</details>

**Environment**:

- Dask version: dask=2023.8.2a230825, distributed=2023.8.2a230825
- Python version: 3.9.17
- Operating System: Ubuntu 20.04
- Install method (conda, pip, source): conda

If so, then the benchmark from the PR's description must be `a = da.from_array(x, chunks=(100, dimy))` (instead of `chunks=(100, 100)`), it errors out instead. Doing that and using Dask's random state to generate data and persisting the data before compute, I seem to only get mildly better results:

![Dask+NumPy+compute()](https://github.com/dask/dask/assets/4398246/5d4ad395-61f6-4253-8440-21b9018cdbee)

However, if I use `persist()` instead of `compute()` the results are now more unstable and difficult to draw a conclusion but seem worse:

![Dask+NumPy+persist()](https://github.com/dask/dask/assets/4398246/f198c94e-3e27-418f-9650-df52afcc6cd7)

I was mainly attempting to see how that would perform for CuPy, but simply replacing NumPy arrays by CuPy arrays I see both with equivalent performance:

![Dask+CuPy+compute()](https://github.com/dask/dask/assets/4398246/0f02f731-4ea1-42fe-a1a6-88d8ec676366)

And with `persist()` I see also similar instability and potentially worse results too:

![Dask+CuPy+persist()](https://github.com/dask/dask/assets/4398246/76838975-1df3-474a-8d4e-5b26960bae43)

The changes look good to me, but the results I've seen above are not conclusively better, it's also possible I did something wrong. For reference, here's the code I ran:

<details><summary>NumPy code</summary>

```python
import dask
import numpy as np
import dask.array as da
import time
from dask.distributed import Client, progress
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


client = Client(processes=False, threads_per_worker=4,
                n_workers=1, memory_limit='4GB')
exponent = np.arange(4,9)
n_elem = np.power(10, exponent)
dimx = 100
t_da = np.zeros(len(n_elem))
t_da_chunks = np.zeros(len(n_elem))
niter = 10
i = 0
for n in n_elem:
    dimy = int(n / dimx)
    rs = da.random.RandomState(RandomState=np.random.RandomState)
    a = rs.randint(n, size=(dimx, dimy), chunks=(100, dimy)).persist()
    for ni in np.arange(niter):
        tic = time.perf_counter_ns()
        a.ravel().persist()
        toc = time.perf_counter_ns()
        if ni:
            t_da[i] += (toc - tic)
    for ni in np.arange(niter):
        tic = time.perf_counter_ns()
        a.ravel_chunks().persist()
        toc = time.perf_counter_ns()
        if ni:
            t_da_chunks[i] += (toc - tic)
    i += 1


client.close()

t_np /= niter - 1
t_da /= niter - 1
t_da_chunks /= niter - 1
print(f"Numpy ravel: {t_np}")
print(f"Dask ravel: {t_da}")
print(f"Dask ravel by chunk: {t_da_chunks}")

# plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

plt.xscale('log')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.xlabel('number of elements')
plt.ylabel('time in nsec')

ax.plot(n_elem, t_da, linestyle='-', marker='o', label='ravel')
ax.plot(n_elem, t_da_chunks, linestyle='-', marker='o', label='ravel_chunks')
ax.legend()

plt.show()
```

</details>

plt.show()

<details><summary>I have used this script to perform a basic benchmarking</summary>

```py
import dask
import numpy as np
import dask.array as da
import time
from dask.distributed import Client, progress
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


client = Client(processes=False, threads_per_worker=4,
                n_workers=1, memory_limit='4GB')
exponent = np.arange(4,9)
n_elem = np.power(10, exponent)
dimx = 100
t_np = np.zeros(len(n_elem))
t_da = np.zeros(len(n_elem))
t_da_chunks = np.zeros(len(n_elem))
niter = 10
i = 0
for n in n_elem:
    dimy = int(n / dimx)
    x = np.random.randint(n, size=(dimx, dimy))
    a = da.from_array(x, chunks=(100,100))
    for ni in np.arange(niter):
        tic = time.perf_counter_ns()
        x.ravel()
        toc = time.perf_counter_ns()
        if ni:
            t_np[i] += (toc - tic)
    for ni in np.arange(niter):
        tic = time.perf_counter_ns()
        a.ravel().compute()
        toc = time.perf_counter_ns()
        if ni:
            t_da[i] += (toc - tic)
    for ni in np.arange(niter):
        tic = time.perf_counter_ns()
        a.ravel_chunks().compute()
        toc = time.perf_counter_ns()
        if ni:
            t_da_chunks[i] += (toc - tic)
    i += 1


client.close()

t_np /= niter - 1
t_da /= niter - 1
t_da_chunks /= niter - 1
print(f"Numpy ravel: {t_np}")
print(f"Dask ravel: {t_da}")
print(f"Dask ravel by chunk: {t_da_chunks}")

# plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

plt.xscale('log')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.xlabel('number of elements')
plt.ylabel('time in nsec')

ax.plot(n_elem, t_da, linestyle='-', marker='o', label='ravel')
ax.plot(n_elem, t_da_chunks, linestyle='-', marker='o', label='ravel_chunks')
ax.legend()

plt.show()
```

</details>

The results are depending on the performances of the machine I used: they are plateauing at arrays with 10^8 elements.

{"action":"opened","number":7950,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/pulls/7950","id":1408572122,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAocYk85T9Rra","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/pull/7950","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/pull/7950.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/pull/7950.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/7950","number":7950,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Fix flaky UCX tests","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Closes #7944

Hardcoding ports can cause binding to fail, thus switching to dynamic ports should make `test_ucx_config_w_env_var`  more reliable.

Additionally, some UCX tests (namely `test_simple` and `test_transpose`) may fail when running from gpuCI's Docker container, even though those are not reproducible on the host where Docker is running. This behavior probably occurs due to the limitation of resources in the container, thus we reduce number of workers and threads to some of the UCX tests, which will make them unlikely to fail while still testing basic UCX functionality.

- [ ] Tests added / passed
- [ ] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

#7944

Hardcoding ports can cause binding to fail, thus switching to dynamic ports should make `test_ucx_config_w_env_var`  more reliable.

Additionally, some UCX tests (namely `test_simple` and `test_transpose`) may fail when running from gpuCI's Docker container, even though those are not reproducible on the host where Docker is running. This behavior probably occurs due to the limitation of resources in the container, thus we reduce number of workers and threads to some of the UCX tests, which will make them unlikely to fail while still testing basic UCX functionality.

- [ ] Tests added / passed
- [ ] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

```
$ mamba create -n cuda102 python=3.8 cudatoolkit=10.2 ucx=1.14
...
Encountered problems while solving:
  - package ucx-1.14.0-h0aa22dc_1 has constraint cudatoolkit 10.2 conflicting with cudatoolkit-10.2.89-h6bb024c_0
```

And for CUDA 11.1, which seems to be an [explicit build of the UCX package](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/main/.ci_support/linux_64_c_compiler_version10cdt_namecos7cuda_compiler_version11.1cxx_compiler_version10.yaml#L14), shows that it's also pinning to 10.2 as well:

```
$ mamba create -n cuda111 python=3.8 cudatoolkit=11.1 ucx=1.14
...
Encountered problems while solving:
  - package ucx-1.14.0-h0aa22dc_1 has constraint cudatoolkit 10.2 conflicting with cudatoolkit-11.1.1-h6406543_8
```

It seems that pinning to `cudatoolkit =={{ cuda_compiler_version }}.*` would resolve this, but I'm honestly not very experienced, also not sure if it would resolve the 11.1 build either. @jakirkham maybe you could shed some light on this?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1531053723/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/7824","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/7824/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/7824/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/distributed/issues/7824/events","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/distributed/issues/7824","id":1703588683,"node_id":"I_kwDOAocYk85lirNL","number":7824,"title":"Can we get rid of sharding?","user":{"login":"crusaderky","id":6213168,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMTMxNjg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6213168?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky","html_url":"https://github.com/crusaderky","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/crusaderky/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-05-10T10:18:21Z","updated_at":"2023-05-10T11:43:16Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"COLLABORATOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The `distributed.comm.shard` setting, which defaults to 64 MiB, is supposed to split a buffer that is larger than the shard size into separate buffers. The intent is to prevent issues that commonly happen beyond 2 GiB with various compression and network protocols.

This setting does nothing for dask.dataframe:

```python
>>> import numpy
>>> import pandas
>>> from distributed.protocol import serialize_bytelist
>>> a = numpy.random.random((2**23,2))  # 128 MiB
>>> frames = serialize_bytelist(a)
>>> [memoryview(f).nbytes for f in frames]
[32, 236, 67108864, 67108864]
>>> frames = serialize_bytelist(pandas.DataFrame(a))
>>> [memoryview(f).nbytes for f in frames]
[40, 123, 554, 134217728, 0]
```

This should be a straightforward bug to fix. However, it also strongly indicates that the whole system tries to solve a purely hypothetical problem. No dask.dataframe user, to my knowledge, has ever complained about crashes.

Do we have an inventory of protocols that break beyond 2 GiB, and/or create temporary deep copies of whole buffers while they work on them?

Sharding causes major performance problems with compression, where any algorithm that doesn't support a `decompress_into` function - _all currently supported algorithms; the only ones I know that do are cramjam and blosc2_ - is plagued by deep-copies and memory spikes because of this:
- #7433

Sharding also adds complexity to the already bloated serialization layer.

- [x] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

There were reports of regression in da.random usage - see https://github.com/coiled/benchmarks/pull/790 for an example. This was surprising because some care were taken for backwards compatibility, but there were some changes and one such change was the introduction of `_make_api` function in `dask/array/random.py` via c4d35f5515:

```
def _make_api(attr, state_constructor=None, state_class=None):
    state_constructor = state_constructor or RandomState
    state_class = state_class or RandomState

    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        backend = array_creation_dispatch.backend
        key = (backend, state_constructor.__name__)
        if key not in _cached_states:
            # Cache the default RandomState object for this backend
            _cached_states[key] = state_constructor()
        return getattr(
            _cached_states[key],
            attr,
        )(*args, **kwargs)

    wrapper.__name__ = getattr(state_class, attr).__name__
    wrapper.__doc__ = getattr(state_class, attr).__doc__
    return wrapper
```


And indeed reverting `_make_api` seems to make the errors go away. Current working hypothesis is there is a race condition in the wrapper function above in lines 8-10: `if key not in dict: dict[key] = "new.rs.object"`. time of check/time of use. This can result in multiple threads creating their own RandomState instances instead of one global instance which can cause havoc down the line - different bound methods with potentially same id - when we call getattr in _apply_random.

@rjzamora : Does that make sense at all? Was there an overwhelming reason for the introduction of _make_api? And perhaps more importantly, any objections for the revert?

I would also like to take this opportunity not to expose Generator based functions directly in `da.random`. There are only 2 such functions - `integers` and `multivariate_hypergeometric` - and they were recently introduced in version 2023.2.1. It was a mistake and we should have never exposed them. One needs to call `default_rng` (as documented) to get a Generator instance and use its methods instead. This is a breaking change but, I believe, well worth the cost.

Any comments are more than welcome.

cc: @jrbourbeau To be clear, "lazy-load" still loads shared libraries at the time of `import cupy`, but using Python code (SoftLink) instead of OS's dynamic loader, to implement Python-level error handling when CUDA libraries could not be found.
> 
> After this task is complete, users can do `import cupy` and use a very limited set of features (e.g., use decorators like `@cupy.memoize` in a global context, use `cupy.ndarray` as an argument signature for mypy, etc.), without installing libraries you listed.

Makes sense, thanks for confirming.

> Providing fine-grained set of features based on libraries available (e.g. disable sparse features if `libcusparse` is missing) is out of the scope of this PR, although it might be implemented in future.

When you say providing fine-grained set of/disabling features, what exactly do you mean? Hiding the relevant API, providing information to the use on what's available somehow, or something else? My understanding is that you won't indeed be able to run any of those if libraries are missing, so I guess CuPy will need anyway to fail somehow when the user attempts to call one of those functions.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/comments/1543697933/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Checklist
- [x] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [x] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [x] Source is from official source.
- [x] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [x] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [x] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [x] Build number is 0.
- [x] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [x] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [x] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Checklist
- [x] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [x] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [x] Source is from official source.
- [x] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [x] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [x] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [x] Build number is 0.
- [x] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [x] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [x] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

<hr>

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Checklist
- [x] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [x] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [x] Source is from official source.
- [x] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [x] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [x] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [x] Build number is 0.
- [x] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [x] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [x] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

<hr>

[the ABI tracker results]( https://abi-laboratory.pro/?view=timeline&l=rdma-core ), `rdma-core` seems fairly stable, but it has broken ABI on major versions in the past (namely 17). {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58/events","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58","id":536554807,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1MzY1NTQ4MDc=","number":58,"title":"Using intrinsics","user":{"login":"jakirkham","id":3019665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMwMTk2NjU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3019665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham","html_url":"https://github.com/jakirkham","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2019-12-11T18:57:03Z","updated_at":"2023-05-23T08:47:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"UCX allows building with intrinsics. According to UCX devs this improves performance slightly (a few percent). These include SSE and AVX options (`./configure` flags below). The Conda compilers usually enable these intrinsics in the compilations flags (along with a fallback for CPUs that don't support these intrinsics). If these flags are simply passing the compiler options we already pass, then enabling them through `./configure` won't have an impact. However if UCX is doing something more clever, these flags may be needed in order to ensure intrinsics are added where UCX devs want them. We should investigate the impact of these flags and enable them if it makes sense.

```
  --with-avx              Use AVX compiler option.
  --with-sse41            Use SSE 4.1 compiler option.
  --with-sse42            Use SSE 4.2 compiler option.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58/events","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58","id":536554807,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1MzY1NTQ4MDc=","number":58,"title":"Using intrinsics","user":{"login":"jakirkham","id":3019665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMwMTk2NjU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3019665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham","html_url":"https://github.com/jakirkham","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakirkham/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2019-12-11T18:57:03Z","updated_at":"2023-05-23T16:08:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"UCX allows building with intrinsics. According to UCX devs this improves performance slightly (a few percent). These include SSE and AVX options (`./configure` flags below). The Conda compilers usually enable these intrinsics in the compilations flags (along with a fallback for CPUs that don't support these intrinsics). If these flags are simply passing the compiler options we already pass, then enabling them through `./configure` won't have an impact. However if UCX is doing something more clever, these flags may be needed in order to ensure intrinsics are added where UCX devs want them. We should investigate the impact of these flags and enable them if it makes sense.

```
  --with-avx              Use AVX compiler option.
  --with-sse41            Use SSE 4.1 compiler option.
  --with-sse42            Use SSE 4.2 compiler option.
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1559747431","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58#issuecomment-1559747431","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/58","id":1559747431,"node_id":"IC_kwDODBZhKc5c99tn","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-23T16:08:52Z","updated_at":"2023-05-23T16:08:52Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Yes, you're right. I thought AVX specifically was not available for AMD CPUs, but it seems what I thought only applies to [AVX-512 which only got supported by AMD Zen4 CPUs last year](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AVX-512#CPUs_with_AVX-512). [AVX is supported by both Intel and AMD CPUs for over a decade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_Vector_Extensions#CPUs_with_AVX), and [AVX2 for roughly a decade by Intel but only since 2015 by AMD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_Vector_Extensions#CPUs_with_AVX2).

I ran some benchmarks:

<details><summary>AVX Enabled</summary>

While the AVX tests with no NVLink nor InfiniBand enabled (where `UCX_TLS` doesn't include `cuda_ipc` and `rc`, respectively) look marginally better the numbers don't seem conclusive as they're somewhat noisy. I've found out that [AVX replaces SSE 4.1/4.2](https://github.com/openucx/ucx/blob/6eb820be5caf7ecd1284ced7ca43e67948aff641/config/m4/compiler.m4#L338-L358), thus I didn't run any separate tests.

With the above said, I don't find the numbers very compelling, therefore I have no strong opinions on enabling this or not. If we decide to do this I would at least wait until https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/pull/125 is in and we have some user reports back, just to avoid making it more difficult to isolate which one of the changes creates problem if anything happens.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1559747431/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Update UCX to build CUDA 12 from new packaging format and CUDA 10/11 with the legacy cudatoolkit package. Not all CUDA 12 packages are available in conda-forge yet, nevertheless we would like to prepare the build for CUDA 12, thus the channel is being changed `nvidia` but this PR should not be merged as is. ``Generator`` internally instead of ``RandomState`` for random number generation. ``Generator`` based random number generation was introduced via e2c74 and brings improvements compared to now-maintenance-only `RandomState` class. Basically we
- use `da.random.default_rng()` to create a `Generator` based random number generator
- make sure we use the correct distribution names for the new `Generator` class (`randint` -> `integers` and so on)

See https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html for further details.

- [x] Tests added / passed
- [x] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

Backport-Candidate","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"PRs tagged should be backported"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-03-09T22:15:16Z","updated_at":"2023-03-10T13:11:54Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

If you call `numpy.savez_compressed` with the keyword `like`, i.e. if one of the named arrays is named "like", it does not get saved to the `npz` file. Nothing like this is documented in the [`savez_compressed` documentation](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.savez_compressed.html). I didn't see an issue that discussed this.

You can unzip the `npz` to verify that the array isn't being saved.

This seems to have started happening somewhere between `numpy==1.19.5` and `numpy==1.21.6`. That is, `numpy==1.19.5` would correctly save the array named "like". Below I used `numpy==1.23.2` and I've also tried `numpy==1.24.2` which behave the same as each other.

I suspect `savez_compressed` isn't the only affected function and "like" isn't the only relevant keyword, but I haven't explored this.

While I'd prefer input to `savez_compressed` to not just be silently ignored, I'd also be okay with just a documentation update to the relevant functions that explained which keywords couldn't be used.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

print(f"numpy.version.full_version={np.version.full_version}")
inputs = {
    "foo": np.array([1], dtype=np.float32),
    "like": np.array([2], dtype=np.float32),
}
print(inputs)
np.savez_compressed("example.npz", **inputs)
loaded_inputs = np.load("example.npz", allow_pickle=True)
print(dict(loaded_inputs))
if inputs != dict(loaded_inputs):
    print("Unexpected mismatch")
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Runtime information:

3.10.9 (main, Dec 19 2022, 17:35:49) [GCC 12.2.0]

But that doesn't seem appropriate for the saving functions. While they might need to be overridden to allow dispatching to other API's implementations of them, these only consume arrays and do not create them, so they should not go through the code path that adds `like=` keyword arguments.

Agreed, this seems very much like a side-effect from NEP-35 and shouldn't be happening. The `like=` kwarg is not explicitly implemented for `np.savez_compressed` though, as you rightly mentioned it wouldn't make sense there, the functions that actively implement `like=` are listed in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/ea0b1708b27e683201285da53c6afda24bc8ba74/numpy/core/tests/test_overrides.py#L567-L587, thus my guess is this is happening due to the use of one of those in the `savez_compressed` implementation. I'll take a look at the implementation to see if I can make sense of what is happening.

> @pentschev @shoyer From the `git blame`, it seems like you two did much of the relevant implementations here. What do you think? This is a bug that should be fixed.

@shoyer has worked on the NEP-18, but NEP-35 is probably all me.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1463785924/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Backport-Candidate","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"PRs tagged should be backported"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2023-03-09T22:15:16Z","updated_at":"2023-03-10T14:03:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

If you call `numpy.savez_compressed` with the keyword `like`, i.e. if one of the named arrays is named "like", it does not get saved to the `npz` file. Nothing like this is documented in the [`savez_compressed` documentation](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.savez_compressed.html). I didn't see an issue that discussed this.

You can unzip the `npz` to verify that the array isn't being saved.

This seems to have started happening somewhere between `numpy==1.19.5` and `numpy==1.21.6`. That is, `numpy==1.19.5` would correctly save the array named "like". Below I used `numpy==1.23.2` and I've also tried `numpy==1.24.2` which behave the same as each other.

I suspect `savez_compressed` isn't the only affected function and "like" isn't the only relevant keyword, but I haven't explored this.

While I'd prefer input to `savez_compressed` to not just be silently ignored, I'd also be okay with just a documentation update to the relevant functions that explained which keywords couldn't be used.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

print(f"numpy.version.full_version={np.version.full_version}")
inputs = {
    "foo": np.array([1], dtype=np.float32),
    "like": np.array([2], dtype=np.float32),
}
print(inputs)
np.savez_compressed("example.npz", **inputs)
loaded_inputs = np.load("example.npz", allow_pickle=True)
print(dict(loaded_inputs))
if inputs != dict(loaded_inputs):
    print("Unexpected mismatch")
```

``Generator`` internally instead of ``RandomState`` for random number generation. ``Generator`` based random number generation was introduced via e2c74 and brings improvements compared to now-maintenance-only `RandomState` class. Basically we
- use `da.random.default_rng()` to create a `Generator` based random number generator
- make sure we use the correct distribution names for the new `Generator` class (`randint` -> `integers` and so on)

See https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html for further details.

- [x] Tests added / passed
- [x] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

seems that the CentOS 6 builds fail with UCX 1.14 because `IBV_ACCESS_ON_DEMAND` is missing as it was previously defined in https://github.com/openucx/ucx/blob/c0aa76d0c121445b8665dbb16e1577ef5ebc6200/src/uct/ib/base/ib_verbs.h#L160-L169 and now is expected to be defined as part of rdma-core. Similar to https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/dd05d5af3a6c4902093ecff980564b6605633124/recipe/install_ucx.sh#L11, I believe we should be building against rdma-core v28 for MOFED 5.x compatibility, which we can't because CentOS 7 goes only up to v22. My suggestion would be to remove [`--with-verbs`](https://github.com/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/blob/dd05d5af3a6c4902093ecff980564b6605633124/recipe/install_ucx.sh#L23) entirely until we can guarantee rdma-core v28 or higher is available as part of the conda-forge packaging process.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1468282499/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

we track `Array._meta` functionality that did not get in with #4543, but should still be implemented.

- [ ] Pass `meta` to all `Array()` constructors where `dtype` does not suffice
  - [ ] `dask/array/core.py` (incomplete, must include fix for #4929)
  - [ ] `dask/array/creation.py` (must include fixes for #4841, #4842)
  - [ ] `dask/array/percentile.py`
  - [ ] `dask/array/reductions.py` (incomplete)
  - [ ] `dask/array/routines.py` (incomplete)
  - [ ] `dask/array/slicing.py`
  - [ ] `dask/array/wrap.py`
  - [ ] `dask/array/tests/test_array_core.py`
  - [ ] `dask/array/tests/test_atop.py`
  - [ ] `dask/array/tests/test_percentiles.py`
  - [ ] `dask/array/tests/test_slicing.py`
  - [ ] `dask/dataframe/core.py`
  - [ ] `dask/dataframe/io/tests/test_io.py`
  - [ ] `docs/source/array-design.rst` (update docs)


- [ ] Eventually move all NumPy-specific array creation to match in use array type (discussion in #4883, initial implementation of NEP-35 in #6738 )
  - [ ] `np.empty`, `np.full`, `np.ones`, `np.zeros`
  - [ ] `np.array`
  - [ ] `np.asarray`
  - [ ] Others?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/4888/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/4888/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1469507979","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4888#issuecomment-1469507979","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/4888","id":1469507979,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAbcwm85XlumL","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-15T07:48:42Z","updated_at":"2023-03-15T07:48:42Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"As we covered most of the common use cases in Dask, we're unlikely to move the needle in this tracker, mostly doing some work in the eventuality of users needing support for specific functions. Tracking","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":242862305,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyNDI4NjIzMDU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/labels/array","name":"array","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2019-04-24T16:12:19Z","updated_at":"2023-03-15T07:49:43Z","closed_at":"2023-03-15T07:49:43Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We want to track what are the issues that need to be solved in the long-term for NEP-18 support and interoperability with libraries such as CuPy.

Structural changes:
- [ ] Support `dask.Array._meta` attribute https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/4543 #4888 
- [ ] `__array_function__` fallback to NumPy implementation https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/12974, https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/13305

Functional changes:
- [x] Support for `shape=` on `*_like functions
  - [x] NumPy https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/13046
  - [x] CuPy https://github.com/cupy/cupy/pull/2171
  - [x] Dask https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4875
- [x] Special empty slice copying in https://github.com/cupy/cupy/pull/2079, requires environment variable `CUPY_EXPERIMENTAL_SLICE_COPY=1`
- [x] Support for `__array_function__` on Dask https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4563

Bug fixes:
- [x] `da.einsum` doesn't work with CuPy arrays #4898 
- [x] `diag()` fails on CuPy arrays https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4490
- [x] prevent `fix()` depending on CuPy's `__array_wrap__` https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4496
- [x] Dispatch modules with `__array_function__` https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/2011
- [x] Multi-threaded support for CuPy on CUDA libraries requiring handles https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4487, https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/2045
- [x] `mean()`/`moment()` reduction issues https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4481, https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4523
- [x] `flatnonzero()` requires `compress()` which is not implemented in CuPy https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4497
- [x] Infinite recursion on CuPy `isclose()` https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/2029
- [x] `da.pad` support: https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/6117
- [x] `da.fft` support: https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/6118

Testing:
- [ ] Increase `__array_function__` testing https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/4486","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/4731/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/4731/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1469510863","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/4731#issuecomment-1469510863","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/4731","id":1469510863,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAbcwm85XlvTP","user":{"login":"pentschev","id":4398246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzOTgyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4398246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev","html_url":"https://github.com/pentschev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pentschev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-15T07:49:43Z","updated_at":"2023-03-15T07:49:43Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think so, we've covered the vast majority of things that we thought were relevant at time of writing. We are unlikely to resolve the last few remaining points as most of the common use cases are now covered in Dask, therefore closing it seems ok.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1469510863/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

The UCX shared libraries depend on symbols from libnuma, however, libnuma is not automatically installed with UCX, and it is not always provided by the base system.
```
$ nm --undefined-only --demangle ~/mambaforge/pkgs/ucx-1.13.1-h30ec399_0/lib/libucs.so | rg numa
                 U numa_all_nodes_ptr@libnuma_1.2
                 U numa_allocate_cpumask@libnuma_1.2
                 U numa_bitmask_free@libnuma_1.2
                 U numa_bitmask_isbitset@libnuma_1.2
                 U numa_max_node@libnuma_1.1
                 U numa_node_to_cpus@libnuma_1.2
                 U numa_num_configured_cpus@libnuma_1.2
```

I guess this now resolves the need for multiple builds. Since we don't need `ucx-proc` anymore, should we add it as a conflict as well? Thinking a bit more about this, maybe this won't be a good idea as it would prevent any users that currently have `ucx-proc` as a dependency (which I believe are all of them) from installing the new package. Instead we may need to add some sort of deprecation warning (assuming this is possible) in `ucx-proc` and remove it for 1.15. Thoughts?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1469839882/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

We could [switch to CentOS 7](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html#using-centos-7) if that helps It helps in the sense that it would allow the build to complete with `--with-verbs`, which doesn't necessarily say anything about the stability of the package with InfiniBand. We have two questions in that sense: 1. Is there anyone who relies on CentOS 6 that prevents us from dropping it? 2. Is anybody absolutely depending on the `--with-verbs` flag? Personally, I have had far too many problems in the past due to mismatching versions related to verbs, and I would definitely prefer to drop `--with-verbs`. IMO, it's better not to have that feature than to potentially have people finding issues that are very difficult to confirm are caused by mismatching version (like I did more than once). Once again, if nobody has objections I would prefer dropping `--with-verbs` and keeping CentOS 6.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1470887520/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Would it be helpful to have an environment variable to enable/disable verbs usage? This can be disabled via `UCX_TLS`, but the problem is when some errors occur there's no way of knowing a potential incompatibility was the source, thus wasting the user's time. Finally, from [UCX's README](https://github.com/openucx/ucx#supported-transports): > NOTE: UCX >= 1.12.0 requires rdma-core >= 28.0 or MLNX_OFED >= 5.0 for [Infiniband](https://www.infinibandta.org/) and [RoCE](http://www.roceinitiative.org/) transports support. This is properly documented, offering a build with rdma-core v22 is IMO incorrect, even if that compiles.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/ucx-split-feedstock/issues/comments/1471498331/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
